frameworks.name: Financial Independence Stack
frameworks.description: Financial independence is not a single threshold — it is a stack of progressively expanding freedom levels. Each level unlocks new agency. Most people aim only at the final level and never reach any.
steps: Level 1 — Financial Safety: 6 months expenses in liquid savings. No high-interest debt. Covers basic protection from disruption.
Level 2 — Financial Stability: income covers all expenses with meaningful surplus. Multiple income streams beginning to form. No financial emergency possible.
Level 3 — Financial Flexibility: passive or semi-passive income covers 20-30% of expenses. Can take selective risks (turn down bad clients, take sabbatical, pursue opportunities).
Level 4 — Financial Independence (FI): passive income covers all expenses. Work is optional. Typically 25x annual expenses invested (4% withdrawal rate).
Level 5 — Financial Freedom: passive income significantly exceeds expenses. Capital allocation at scale. Generational impact possible.
Identify your current level and the specific gap to the next level — not the final level
antipatterns: Targeting Level 4/5 while neglecting Level 1/2 — the base is not optional
Lifestyle inflation that moves the target faster than the portfolio grows
Single income stream dependence — one failure point is not a stack

frameworks.name: FI Number Calculation
frameworks.description: Financial independence has a specific number. Most people avoid calculating it because the number feels large. Knowing the number creates a target; not knowing it creates a fog.
steps: Calculate your annual expenses: actual spending over last 12 months, not estimated
Determine your FI target: annual expenses × 25 (4% safe withdrawal rate). This is your FI number.
Calculate your current gap: FI number minus current investable assets
Calculate years to FI at current savings rate: use compound growth calculator with conservative 7% real return
Identify the highest-leverage variable: savings rate (controlled by you) matters more than return rate
Model the impact of savings rate changes: increasing savings rate from 20% to 40% roughly halves your FI timeline
antipatterns: Using estimated expenses rather than actual — consistently understates the real number
Ignoring inflation — a static FI number erodes over time
Optimizing return rate over savings rate — return is not controlled; savings rate is

frameworks.name: Income Architecture for Independence
frameworks.description: Financial independence is reached faster with multiple income streams that include at least one with leverage. Salary alone, regardless of amount, produces linear progress.
steps: Audit current income sources: salary, consulting, business, investments, royalties, rental
Identify leverage ratio of each: time-for-money (1:1) vs. leveraged (1:many)
Build one leveraged income stream before optimizing any existing streams — the multiplier effect dominates
Sequence: earned income (high flow) → invest surplus → build business income → acquire assets → live on asset income
Protect the primary income engine during transition — do not quit the salary before the replacement is proven
Calculate your crossover point: the moment passive income exceeds expenses — this is financial independence
antipatterns: Quitting salary before replacement income is established — destroys the safety stack
Multiple income streams all at 1:1 leverage — diversification without leverage is just multiple jobs
No investment of surplus — earned income without investment never becomes passive income

prompts.name: Financial Independence Stack Audit
prompts.template: Audit my financial independence stack. Monthly expenses: [amount]. Emergency fund: [months]. High-interest debt: [amount]. Income sources and leverage ratio: [list]. Current investable assets: [amount]. Identify my current FI level, my FI number, my current gap, and the highest-leverage action to advance to the next level.

prompts.name: FI Number Calculation
prompts.template: Calculate my FI number and timeline. Annual expenses (actual): [amount]. Current investable assets: [amount]. Monthly savings rate: [amount]. Expected return: [% or use 7%]. Show me: FI number, current gap, years to FI at current rate, and the impact of increasing savings rate by 10%.

prompts.name: Income Architecture Design
prompts.template: Design my income architecture for financial independence. Current income: [describe]. Skills and assets I could leverage: [describe]. Time available outside primary income: [hours/week]. Target FI timeline: [years]. Give me a sequenced income architecture with leverage ratios and transition timing.

examples.title: Accelerating from Level 2 to Level 3
examples.context: Salaried professional at Level 2 (stable, no debt, small savings). Annual expenses $60K. FI number $1.5M. Current assets $200K.
examples.intervention: Built productized consulting service alongside salary generating $2K/month passive. Increased savings rate from 20% to 35% by eliminating lifestyle inflation.
examples.result: FI timeline reduced from 22 years to 14 years. Level 3 (flexibility) reached in 4 years — enough passive income to turn down low-value work.

vocabulary.definition: Annual expenses multiplied by 25. The investable asset level at which a 4% annual withdrawal covers all expenses indefinitely.
The percentage of portfolio withdrawn annually with high historical probability of not depleting capital over a 30-year horizon. Conventionally 4%.
The moment passive income exceeds monthly expenses. The definition of financial independence reached.
Percentage of income saved and invested. The most controllable variable in FI timeline. Increasing from 20% to 40% roughly halves the timeline.
Income generated at a ratio greater than 1:1 effort-to-output. Content, software, and productized services are examples. The structural prerequisite for financial independence.
The tendency to increase spending proportionally with income increases. Eliminates the savings rate gains from income growth.
The stage where passive income covers 20-30% of expenses. Unlocks genuine agency: ability to decline bad opportunities without financial panic.

v78_consciousnesses: Constraint Mapper (self-imposed vs real financial constraints)
master_laws: Law 3 (Agency — FI is the structural foundation of agency)
Law 19 (Compound — FI is applied compound interest)
Law 9 (Time — timeline is the key variable)
integration_hooks.caveat: Strategic framework only. Not financial advice. Consult a fee-only fiduciary for personalized financial planning.

4_gate_validation.safe: PASS — caveat embedded; no specific investment recommendations
4_gate_validation.true: PASS — 4% withdrawal rate and 25x multiplier are well-documented
4_gate_validation.high_leverage: PASS — FI architecture directly produces structural agency
